data.title: Friday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
08:30 AM Employment Situation 

The most closely watched of all economic indicators, the employment situation is a set of monthly labor market indicators based on two separate reports: the establishment survey and the household survey. 

1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count 

The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs.

data.title: DocuSign raises FY26 revenue midpoint by $38M as IAM adoption accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: DocuSign (DOCU) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Allan Thygesen, President, CEO & Director, described Q2 as "an outstanding quarter" and highlighted that platform innovation, combined with go-to-market changes introduced in Q1, drove strong performance in commercial and enterprise segments across eSignature, CLM, and the AI-native Docusign Intelligent Agreement Management (IAM) platform. Thygesen reported, "Q2 business results outperformed our expectations. Revenue was $801 million, up 9% year-over-year, and billings were $818 million, up 13% year-over-year."
  • Dollar net retention increased to 102% due to higher gross retention rates. The company saw steady growth in envelopes sent, improved contract utilization, and accelerated international growth, with digital revenue outpacing the overall business. IAM customers represented a greater share of direct deal volume and gross bookings than in Q1, and commercial SMB customers continued their strong pace of IAM investment.
  • Thygesen announced a new partnership with the U.S. Federal Government’s General Services Administration (GSA) and highlighted the largest Q2 deal transacted through the Microsoft Azure marketplace.
  • On product innovation, Thygesen stated, "In Q2, we launched several new AI-powered IAM capabilities to help customers unlock value across the entire agreement management lifecycle," including Custom Extractions and Agreement Preparation.
  • Thygesen acknowledged changes to the Board, welcoming James Beer as Board Chair and Mike Rosenbaum as a new member.
  • Blake Grayson, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Our performance was strong across the business in Q2, a testament to our continued execution against our three strategic pillars." Grayson noted, "Non-GAAP gross margin for Q2 was 82.0%, relatively in line with the prior year, as higher revenue mostly offset the impact of cloud migration costs."

Outlook

  • DocuSign expects total revenue between $804 million to $808 million in Q3, reflecting a 7% year-over-year increase at the midpoint. Fiscal 2026 revenue guidance is set at $3.189 billion to $3.201 billion, also a 7% year-over-year increase at the midpoint.
  • Subscription revenue guidance for Q3 is $786 million to $790 million, and $3.121 billion to $3.133 billion for fiscal 2026.
  • Billings guidance for Q3 is $785 million to $795 million, with fiscal 2026 billings forecasted at $3.325 billion to $3.355 billion.
  • Grayson explained, "The annual guidance midpoint is increasing by $38 million from last quarter’s full year guidance," primarily due to Q2 business strength and expectations for continued trends, while billings guidance increases by $28 million. The outlook factors in renewal timing headwinds and a more challenging year-over-year comparison in the second half of fiscal 2026.
  • Non-GAAP gross margin guidance is 80.3% to 81.3% for Q3 and 81.0% to 82.0% for fiscal 2026. Non-GAAP operating margin guidance is 28.0% to 29.0% for Q3 and 28.6% to 29.6% for fiscal 2026.

Financial Results

  • Q2 revenue was $801 million, and subscription revenue was $784 million, both up 9% year-over-year. Billings were $818 million, up 13% year-over-year.
  • Free cash flow was $218 million, representing a 27% margin. DocuSign repurchased $200 million in shares during Q2.
  • Non-GAAP diluted EPS for Q2 was $0.92, while GAAP diluted EPS was $0.30.
  • Dollar net retention rose to 102% in Q2 from 101% in Q1 and 99% in Q2 of the prior year. Total customers grew 9% year-over-year, exceeding 1.7 million, with large customers spending over $300k annually increasing by 7% year-over-year to 1,137.
  • International revenue accounted for 29% of total revenue and grew 13% year-over-year, with Asia Pacific as the fastest-growing international region.

Q&A

  • Robbie Owens, Piper Sandler, asked about improved fundamentals in eSignature. Grayson replied, "This is a trend we've been seeing pretty consistently over the past year," highlighting strong growth in financial services, health care, and business services.
  • Tyler Radke, Citi, inquired about CLM’s breakout quarter and pipeline. Thygesen responded, "I think it was a very strong quarter, and we closed some very large deals, really exciting and great to see."
  • Jacob Roberge, William Blair, asked about IAM adoption in enterprise and international markets. Thygesen said, "We started to see some larger deals with enterprise customers, which is very exciting because that's an even larger market opportunity for us in the long run."
  • Josh Baer, Morgan Stanley, pressed for IAM’s contribution to billings outperformance. Grayson: "IAM did slightly outperform our expectations, but it's still very early days for us in that area and that platform."
  • Bill (for Kirk Materne), Evercore ISI, asked about drivers behind improved gross retention. Thygesen noted, "Operational execution plays a huge part of this. We have folks at Docusign now spending a much greater portion of time with regards to staying in front of these renewal opportunities."
  • Brad Sills, BofA Securities, questioned the impact of the GSA partnership. Thygesen: "Our federal business is relatively modest today... there’s just a lot of headroom."
  • Austin Cole, Citizens JMP, asked about sales team adaptation to go-to-market changes. Thygesen stated, "I feel really good about. Look, we made those changes to position us for the long-term to accelerate our IAM business and just more generally be able to go deeper with customers."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was positive, with questions focusing on growth drivers, IAM adoption, and the impact of partnerships rather than expressing skepticism or pressing on risk factors.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone in prepared remarks, with Thygesen stating, "We believe IAM and the Docusign Iris AI engine are uniquely positioned to transform how organizations operate their businesses."
  • During Q&A, management remained constructive and provided detailed responses, showing a consistent level of confidence compared to the previous quarter.
  • Compared to last quarter, both management and analysts sustained a positive sentiment, with a shift toward greater focus on IAM’s early enterprise traction and improved fundamentals.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue and billings growth accelerated compared to Q1, and management raised full-year revenue and billings guidance midpoints.
  • IAM continued to gain traction, representing a higher percentage of direct deal volume and gross bookings than in Q1.
  • Management’s tone reflected increased confidence, particularly around innovation, go-to-market changes, and the larger deal sizes in enterprise.
  • Analysts shifted from questions on early renewals and timing impacts in Q1 to focus on product adoption, deal momentum, and expansion opportunities in Q2.
  • Operational execution and improved gross retention rates were emphasized more strongly this quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited renewal timing variability as a key factor impacting billings quarter-to-quarter, and is evaluating potential topline reporting changes.
  • Cloud migration continues to be a headwind for gross and operating margins.
  • The federal partnership is still in early stages and not yet a meaningful contributor to revenue.
  • Thygesen acknowledged headroom in federal and enterprise expansion, while Grayson flagged that billings can fluctuate due to contract timing.

Final Takeaway

DocuSign’s Q2 2026 call emphasized accelerating revenue and billings, improved retention, and strong adoption of the IAM platform across both commercial and enterprise customers. Management highlighted raised full-year guidance and ongoing product innovation with AI-powered capabilities, while noting continued margin discipline and active share repurchases. The company expressed confidence in the durability of growth, supported by go-to-market changes, expanding enterprise penetration, and new strategic partnerships, while continuing to monitor renewal timing and cloud migration impacts.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Guidewire expects $1.21B–$1.22B ARR in fiscal 2026 as cloud growth accelerates with major Tier 1 wins
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Guidewire Software, Inc. (GWRE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Mike Rosenbaum emphasized, "We emphatically surpassed the $1 billion ARR milestone and collectively feel great about the future potential in the business." Rosenbaum reported accelerating growth rates in both ARR and fully ramped ARR, highlighting a 19% ARR increase and a 22% fully ramped ARR increase for fiscal 2025 on a constant currency basis. He underscored demand strength for Guidewire Cloud, citing Q4 as a record quarter with the landmark Liberty Mutual deal—where Liberty Mutual will migrate ClaimCenter to cloud and adopt PolicyCenter, making a 10-year commitment. Rosenbaum stated, "This is one of the most strategic partnerships in our history."
  • Rosenbaum noted that Guidewire Cloud margins are improving ahead of schedule, with subscription and support gross margins finishing the year at 70% and described the company as being in a unique strategic position to unlock generative AI potential in the insurance sector.
  • President John Mullen stated, "We continue to see growing demand, signing 19 core cloud deals in Q4, totaling 57 for the year, representing healthy annual growth in deal count." Mullen also highlighted strong geographic performance, including 11 deals in Europe and increased momentum in Latin America and Asia Pacific. He confirmed, "We did 9 deals with Tier 1 brands in the fourth quarter."
  • CFO Jeffrey Cooper said, "ARR ended the year at $1.032 billion, up 19% year-over-year on a constant currency basis and ahead of our expectations." Cooper also reported, "Total revenue for the year was $1.2 billion, ahead of our expectations due to strong performance across all components of revenue." He added, "Operating income was $208 million, up 109% year-over-year and above the high end of our outlook by $13 million."

Outlook

  • For fiscal 2026, Guidewire expects ARR between $1.21 billion and $1.22 billion, representing 17% constant currency growth at the midpoint. The company projects total revenue between $1.385 billion and $1.405 billion. Subscription revenue is expected to be approximately $888 million, a 34% increase, with subscription and support revenue around $945 million. License revenue is anticipated to decline by over $30 million due to accelerated cloud migrations. Services revenue is expected to reach approximately $232 million.
  • Subscription and support gross margins are forecasted at 71%–72% for fiscal 2026, with total gross margins at approximately 66%. Non-GAAP operating income is expected between $259 million and $279 million. Cash flow from operations is projected at $350 million to $370 million.
  • For Q1 2026, the company expects ARR between $1.048 billion and $1.054 billion, subscription and support revenue of approximately $218 million, and services revenue of about $60 million.

Financial Results

  • ARR ended the year at $1.032 billion, with a $9 million FX adjustment resulting in $1.041 billion. Fully ramped ARR grew 22% year-over-year. Total Cloud ARR comprised 74% of total ARR and grew 36% year-over-year.
  • Subscription revenue finished the year at $667 million, up 40% year-over-year, while subscription and support revenue reached $731 million, up 33%. License revenue was $252 million, up 1%. Services revenue rose to $219 million, a 21% increase from the prior year.
  • Non-GAAP gross profit was $789 million, up 28% year-over-year, with overall gross margin at 66%. Operating income reached $208 million and cash flow from operations was $301 million. The company ended the quarter with $1.5 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and investments.

Q&A

  • Rishi Jaluria, RBC: Asked about record low ARR attrition and drivers. CEO Rosenbaum responded, "The company just generally benefits from having an extremely durable customer base... the focus we've had on ensuring project success... benefits our ability to ensure that we continue to renew in that every single one of these contracts."
  • Jaluria followed up on platform expansion post-cloud transition. Rosenbaum said, "Act 3 is all about data and analytics and more innovative application use cases that we can uniquely build."
  • Dylan Becker, William Blair: Questioned the impact of premium growth and pricing efficiency. Rosenbaum explained, "Premium growth, direct written premium growth generally benefits Guidewire but... there may be changes that are going on in the industry related to premium growth that don't directly flow into our revenue model."
  • Alexei Gogolev, JPMorgan: Inquired about competition and DWP trajectory. Rosenbaum stated, "Almost every single customer prospect I speak to recognizes that it would be beneficial to modernize their core system to a platform like Guidewire."
  • Aaron Kimson, Citizens JMP: Sought details on the Liberty Mutual deal term. Rosenbaum said, "I don't see this yet as a pattern for all the conversations that we have with Tier 1s."
  • Hoi-Fung Wong, Oppenheimer: Asked about infusing AI into services. Rosenbaum: "Absolutely yes. It's one of the primary agendas with our services team this year."
  • Tyler Radke, Citi: Asked if Liberty Mutual is the largest cloud deal. Rosenbaum: "I would say this is the most strategic deal we've ever done in the history of the company."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts conveyed a positive tone, focusing on demand drivers, competitive positioning, and the impact of major deals, as well as probing for detail about the strategic direction with AI and cloud.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and answers, often reinforcing statements with phrases like "we feel great" and "we are very, very excited." The tone remained consistent with the previous quarter, though with increased confidence following the Liberty Mutual deal and cloud milestone.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for fiscal 2026 was raised, with explicit projections for ARR and revenue growth. Management’s focus shifted from completing the cloud transition to leveraging data, analytics, and AI, marking a new strategic phase.
  • Analysts’ questions evolved from platform proof points and regional expansion in Q3 to inquiries about large-scale deals, AI use cases, and further platform expansion in Q4.
  • Key financial metrics, such as ARR, revenue, and margins, showed continued improvement, with the Q4 call highlighting acceleration in cloud adoption and profitability.
  • Management’s confidence level increased, with more forward-looking statements about innovation and AI.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the need to continue executing successful cloud migrations while supporting on-prem customers through transition. Rosenbaum stated, "We continue to invest in mechanisms to make that migration more and more smooth and less and less risky."
  • CFO Cooper noted that license revenue is expected to decline due to ongoing migrations and modeled conservatism around true-up activity and pricing in fiscal 2026.
  • Questions around the scalability of AI development and the complexity of maintaining rapid software release cycles were acknowledged as areas of ongoing assessment.

Final Takeaway

Guidewire finished fiscal 2025 with record ARR and revenue, driven by robust demand for its cloud platform and landmark customer wins such as Liberty Mutual. Management forecasts continued double-digit growth in ARR and subscription revenue for fiscal 2026, with improving margins and strong cash flow expected. The company is entering a new strategic phase focused on data, analytics, and AI, and maintains a healthy pipeline and strong market momentum, supported by its expanding ecosystem and referenceable success with leading insurers.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Copart signals continued auction liquidity investment as global ASPs rise 5.6% amid strategic channel shifts
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Copart, Inc. (CPRT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jeffrey Liaw opened the call by stating, "We're pleased to announce the results of another record year for Copart across a number of dimensions, including units sold, revenue and operating profit." Liaw highlighted a 4.5% increase in global insurance volume for the fiscal year and discussed the cyclical disconnect between accident activity and insurance claims frequency, noting, "total loss frequency has continued its long-term upward trend." He emphasized the distinct advantage of Copart's auction liquidity, referencing the company's "uniquely digital" and "uniquely global" auction platform, with international members accounting for approximately 40% of vehicles sold at U.S. auctions.
  • Liaw reported, "for the quarter, we experienced ASP growth globally of 5.4% for all insurance vehicles sold and for our U.S. insurance clients, growth of 5.7% for the fourth quarter versus a year ago."
  • CFO Leah Stearns stated, "For fiscal year '25, global unit sales increased 4.8% and declined in the fourth quarter by 0.9%." Stearns explained the strategic shift of low-value noninsurance units from Copart direct to the direct buy channel, allowing for more efficient marketing and cost avoidance, noting, "they are not captured in our units sold metrics."
  • Stearns highlighted that Copart's Blue Car segment grew 15.3% in fiscal year '25, with ongoing double-digit growth across bank and fleet partners.

Outlook

  • Management described ongoing investments in auction liquidity and digital enhancements as the top operational priority for the new fiscal year, with Liaw stating, "We continue to invest in auction liquidity broadly speaking, and that literally can mean recruiting and retaining members... reducing friction in the member experience itself."
  • Liaw addressed capital allocation, explaining, "over the long haul... we have consistently returned cash to shareholders via buybacks," and confirmed a continued search for M&A opportunities that enhance Copart's core service proposition.

Financial Results

  • Stearns reported, "Global revenue increased to $1.13 billion for the quarter and $4.65 billion for fiscal year '25, reflecting a 5.2% and 9.7% growth, respectively."
  • Global service revenue increased $63.1 million or 7% from the same period last year, and U.S. service revenue grew by 6.2% for the quarter.
  • Facility-related costs in the U.S. increased $13 million or 3.4% for the quarter, with expanded operational capacity cited as a driver.
  • Stearns noted, "Fourth quarter GAAP operating income increased by 14.8% to $412.6 million, and for the fiscal year, GAAP operating income increased by 8% to $1.7 billion."
  • She added, "Fourth quarter GAAP net income attributable to Copart, Inc. increased by 22.9% to $396.4 million or $0.41 per diluted common share."
  • As of the end of July, Copart reported $6 billion of liquidity, including $4.8 billion in cash and held-to-maturity securities.

Q&A

  • Bob Labick, CJS Securities: Asked about technology advancements and AI. Liaw responded that AI is "widely deployed inside Copart today," supporting decision-making, customer support, and auction recommendations, and expects further efficiency and opportunity as "the tools themselves improve."
  • Labick also inquired about electric vehicles (EVs) and total loss frequency. Liaw indicated that EVs "total, if anything, more easily," due to advanced technology and sensors that make repairs more costly.
  • John Healy, Northcoast Research: Sought operational priorities for the new fiscal year. Liaw emphasized, "Auction liquidity enables us to serve our incumbent insurance clients better."
  • Healy queried capital allocation. Liaw reiterated, "Ultimately, the answer is share buybacks without a precise time frame... the cash doesn't cause us to change our behavior either on M&A or on operating expenses."
  • Christopher Bottiglieri, BNP Paribas: Asked about assignment declines and ASP growth. Stearns clarified, "the assignment decline was low single digit, not low double digits." Liaw stated Copart generates "superior auction returns" compared to peers, referencing a 5.7% increase in insurance returns for the quarter.
  • Bret Jordan, Jefferies: Inquired on autonomous vehicles and insurance trends. Liaw said current auction impact from autonomous vehicles is "a de minimis effect" and insurance coverage remains cyclical.
  • Jeffrey Lick, Stephens: Asked about the impact of hurricane season and insurance profitability. Liaw noted storm impact is "difficult to predict in advance" and competitive dynamics among insurers continue to ebb and flow.
  • Jash Patwa, JPMorgan: Asked about market share and growth opportunities. Liaw described the opportunity as "much more expansive," stating Copart faces "many-fold opportunities to win, many-fold opportunities to lose."
  • Patwa also asked about combining select and bank repo auctions. Liaw described it as a "specific tactical experiment," with the goal to "generate the very best returns by matching the right buyers to the right cars."
  • Patwa requested details on a PP&E sale. Stearns said it was a "small equipment sale" and "nonrecurring."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, focusing on technology adoption, capital allocation, and market structure. Questions probed operational priorities, AI, and market share, suggesting interest in Copart's positioning but no overt skepticism.
  • Management maintained a confident and informative tone, offering detailed explanations and highlighting achievements. Liaw frequently referenced long-term trends and strategic focus, expressing confidence in the company's auction liquidity and technology investments.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone remained measured, while management's tone showed consistent confidence but with increased emphasis on digital and operational efficiency initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to emphasize auction liquidity and digital platform enhancements as top priorities, compared to last quarter's focus on storm readiness and physical infrastructure.
  • Strategic focus moved toward technology deployment, efficiency gains, and channel optimization, with more explicit discussion of shifting low-value units to direct channels.
  • Management continued to highlight global ASP growth and superior auction returns, with slightly higher growth figures this quarter (5.6% global ASP increase vs. 3% last quarter).
  • Analysts continued to inquire about technology, insurance trends, and market share, but this quarter included more focus on operational priorities and the impact of AI.
  • Management sentiment remained confident, with more detailed discussion of digital strategies and liquidity investments versus the previous quarter's focus on storm preparedness and regulatory developments.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management pointed to cyclical declines in insurance volumes and assignment growth, attributing softness in the second half to changes in insurance carrier activity and consumer insurance coverage.
  • Facility-related cost increases and ongoing investments in operational capacity were highlighted as potential margin pressures.
  • Uncertainties in the heavy equipment and agricultural sectors were noted, with sellers adopting a "wait-and-see approach" due to macroeconomic uncertainties.
  • No significant one-time items or nonrecurring revenue impacts were identified beyond a minor equipment sale.

Final Takeaway

Copart management credited the company's record performance in units sold, revenue, and operating profit to its global digital auction platform and continued investment in auction liquidity. The quarter saw notable growth in global ASPs, expansion in Blue Car and international segments, and strategic channel shifts to enhance efficiency. Management emphasized operational priorities centered on liquidity and technology, with a robust liquidity position supporting ongoing investment and shareholder returns. The company remains focused on leveraging its digital and global reach to sustain superior auction outcomes amid evolving industry and macroeconomic trends.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Samsara outlines 26% FY26 revenue growth target while accelerating AI-driven customer adoption
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Samsara Inc. (IOT) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Sanjit Biswas highlighted that Samsara ended Q2 with $1.6 billion in ARR, reflecting 30% year-over-year growth. The company achieved a record by adding 17 customers with over $1 million in ARR, now representing more than 20% of total ARR. "Our $100,000-plus ARR customers now contribute close to $1 billion of ARR, up 35% year-over-year and now represent 59% of our total ARR," said Biswas. He emphasized the company’s strategic focus on large enterprises, citing partnerships with Alaska Airlines, SRM Concrete, and a Fortune 1000 rental equipment company.

  • Biswas detailed the launch of several new products at the Samsara Beyond conference, including asset maintenance, commercial navigation, route planning, AI Multicam, and worker safety. He pointed out growing AI adoption among customers and noted a shift toward automation and unified platforms to manage complex operations.

  • The company expanded its partner ecosystem to over 350 integrations and appointed Gary Steele to its Board of Directors, with Biswas stating, "His expertise in enterprise software and AI will be invaluable as we continue to drive multiproduct adoption and deliver clear ROI for our customers."

  • CFO Dominic Phillips reported, "Q2 was another quarter of durable growth and improved profitability." He cited 19% year-over-year net new ARR growth, a quarterly record in new $1 million-plus ARR customers, and 8% of net new ACV from products launched in the past year. Phillips confirmed, "Q2 revenue was $391 million, growing 30% year-over-year or 31% in constant currency."

Outlook

  • Phillips provided Q3 guidance with revenue expected between $398 million and $400 million, representing 24% year-over-year growth. Non-GAAP operating margin is projected at 15% and non-GAAP EPS between $0.11 and $0.12.

  • For full year FY26, the company expects revenue between $1.574 billion and $1.578 billion, representing 26% year-over-year growth, and non-GAAP EPS between $0.45 and $0.47.

  • Management stated, "We believe we are well positioned to deliver durable growth and create long-term shareholder value for a few key reasons," referencing data advantages, AI innovation, and exposure to large operations budgets.

Financial Results

  • Q2 ending ARR was $1.64 billion, an increase of 30% year-over-year. Net new ARR added was $105 million, up 19% year-over-year.

  • Revenue for Q2 was $391 million, up 30% year-over-year. Non-GAAP gross margin reached 78% and non-GAAP operating margin was 15%, both showing year-over-year improvement. Free cash flow margin increased to 11% in Q2, up 7 percentage points year-over-year.

  • The company signed 7 net new $1 million-plus ACV transactions and ended Q2 with 147 $1 million-plus ARR customers. International net new ACV comprised 15% of the total, driven mainly by Europe.

  • Construction, public sector, and manufacturing were highlighted as strong end markets, with construction contributing the highest net new ACV mix for the eighth consecutive quarter.

Q&A

  • Aleksandr Zukin, Wolfe Research: Asked about the traction of new product launches and ACV growth from new products. Biswas responded that products like routing, commercial navigation, and asset tags are seeing positive momentum and pilots across industries, with 8% of new ACV from these offerings.

  • Zukin followed up on the bounce-back in net new ARR after Q1. Phillips explained, "There were a few larger deals...that we originally had forecasted would come in to Q1, but ultimately pushed to Q2 after the liberation day tariff announcements. All of those larger impacted deals closed in Q2."

  • Matthew Hedberg, RBC: Inquired about AI monetization. Biswas indicated that both core and new products are benefiting from AI enhancements and that AI will enable new products and improve existing ones.

  • Christopher Quintero, Morgan Stanley: Asked about investments supporting large deals and European expansion. Biswas described company-wide efforts in sales, implementation, and software for large enterprises. Phillips attributed European growth to sustained investment and region-specific product development.

  • Michael Turrin, Wells Fargo: Queried about AI infrastructure build-out and public sector traction. Biswas stated, "Construction had the highest contribution to our net new ACV mix for the eighth quarter in a row," and discussed the public sector's focus on operational savings and compliance.

  • Analysts repeatedly sought clarity on the basis for large deal momentum, sales productivity, and product portfolio breadth, with management emphasizing strong enterprise traction, balanced sales capacity, and a focus on multiproduct adoption.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was generally positive, with recognition of strong results but questions focused on sustainability, deal timing, and new product ramp. Several expressed interest in how new products and AI will drive future growth and how the company manages complex sales cycles.

  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was confident, with frequent references to records and milestones. During Q&A, management maintained a positive and factual tone, addressing questions directly and acknowledging challenges where relevant. Phillips referenced, "If we don't see those scenarios ultimately play out, it generally results in us being able to outperform the guidance."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analyst sentiment showed increased optimism, with less concern about macro or tariff-related headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q3 and FY26 was raised from Q1 expectations, with full-year revenue growth targeted at 26% versus 24% previously. Non-GAAP operating margin guidance increased to 15% for the year, up from 13% in Q1 guidance.

  • The company achieved a record 17 new $1 million-plus ARR customers in Q2, compared to 154 additions of $100,000-plus ARR customers in Q1. The share of ARR from $1 million-plus customers grew to over 20%.

  • Q2 saw a sequential acceleration in net new ARR growth, attributed in part to the closure of delayed deals from Q1. Management commentary shifted from caution around macro and tariff impact to confidence in customer adaptation and sales execution.

  • Analysts in Q2 focused more on product innovation, AI monetization strategies, and enterprise adoption, compared to the previous quarter’s focus on sales cycle elongation and macro uncertainty.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged possible variability in quarterly results due to the timing of large enterprise deals. Phillips stated, "Larger deals have inherently longer and less predictable sales cycles, which means that their timing may introduce more variability into our quarterly results than in the past."

  • Tariff-related uncertainty was addressed, with Biswas noting, "At this point, there's still uncertainty on the tariff rates themselves, but tariffs appear to be here to stay. What I'm hearing from customers is they've adapted to the environment."

  • The company faces ongoing challenges with customer change management, especially as it introduces more products and expands internationally.

Final Takeaway

Samsara delivered another quarter of strong growth, marked by record new large customers, accelerating adoption of AI-powered products, and improved profitability. Management raised full-year guidance, citing resilience in enterprise demand, successful closure of delayed deals, and positive momentum across key end markets. The company’s focus on multiproduct adoption, AI-driven innovation, and expanding international presence positions it for durable long-term growth, while management remains attentive to risks from large deal variability and evolving macro conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: UiPath raises FY26 revenue guidance to $1.576B while agentic automation momentum builds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: UiPath Inc. (PATH) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Daniel Dines, Co-Founder, CEO, & Executive Chairman of the Board, opened by crediting improved execution and platform innovation for shaping the company's strong financial results, highlighting that "automation and AI are stronger together." He pointed out that "second quarter ARR grew 11% to $1.723 billion, driven by $31 million in net new ARR" and noted that "second quarter revenue was $362 million, an increase of 14% from the prior year period."
  • Dines emphasized that new AI and Agentic solutions are accelerating deal sizes and increasing commercial momentum, stating, "our AI and Agentic solutions are helping us win deals and increase deal sizes faster than traditional automation engagements and now represent a growing share of commercial activity."
  • He detailed recent customer wins leveraging Agentic products, including a "7-figure deal with a Fortune 15 global technology company" and expansion with Voya Financial, as well as new strategic partnerships and deepened alliances with Microsoft and Deloitte.
  • Ashim Gupta, CFO & COO, remarked, "we completed our restructuring and remain focused on driving productivity and disciplined execution," and added, "we ended the quarter with more than $1.08 billion in cloud ARR, which includes both hybrid and SaaS, an increase of more than 25%."

Outlook

  • UiPath is raising its third quarter revenue guidance to a range of $390 million to $395 million and full fiscal year 2026 revenue guidance to a range of $1.571 billion to $1.576 billion. ARR is expected to be between $1.771 billion and $1.776 billion for Q3 and between $1.834 billion and $1.839 billion for the full year. Non-GAAP operating income is guided at approximately $70 million for Q3 and $340 million for the year. Gupta stated, "we are pleased with the team's execution in a variable macroeconomic environment...we continue to maintain a prudent outlook and guide to what we see in front of us."
  • The company reiterated that while there is "early traction with our newly launched Agentic capabilities, adoption is still in its early phases, and as such, we don't expect a material top line contribution in fiscal 2026."

Financial Results

  • Second quarter revenue reached $362 million, with ARR at $1.723 billion. The company reported non-GAAP operating income of $62 million, representing a 17% margin. Dollar-based gross retention was 98%, and dollar-based net retention rate was 108%.
  • Remaining performance obligations increased to $1.209 billion and current RPO to $789 million. Gross margin stood at 84%, and software gross margin at 90%. Operating expenses were $243 million, a reduction of 6% from the prior year.
  • Non-GAAP adjusted free cash flow was $45 million. The company ended the quarter with $1.5 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities and no debt. UiPath repurchased 8.3 million shares at an average price of $12.10.

Q&A

  • Bryan Bergin, TD Cowen, Research Division: Asked about client demand progression for agentic solutions and deal size impact. Dines responded that "having like 450 customers actively working with our technology, building agents with the intention to deploy in production is really a meaningful...impact," and Gupta added, "it reinforces often the need for deterministic automation with our RPA and AI capabilities."
  • Bergin: Asked about DBNRR stability. Gupta replied, "we're stabilizing. That's what we feel. I won't make a commentary at this point to out quarters too far."
  • Jacob Roberge, William Blair: Asked about Maestro's customer pitch and go-to-market health. Dines said, "our Maestro is very tightly integrated with our automation platform...the breadth of our platform...is one of the best in the market today." He described the go-to-market motion as "stable" and "much closer to the customer."
  • Austin Williams, Wells Fargo: Asked about the U.S. federal business and subscription revenue. Gupta noted "public sector had just a really good quarter in terms of the momentum of selling with Agentic" and explained subscription revenue was "back to stable" after a leap year impact last quarter.
  • Raimo Lenschow, Barclays: Inquired about raised guidance and customer adoption patterns. Gupta attributed confidence to "momentum...with both the improvements that Daniel talked about on the go-to-market side as well as Agentic."
  • Other analysts raised questions about Agentic pricing, go-to-market specialization, process prioritization, and sectoral/geographic trends, with management emphasizing ongoing momentum and prudent outlooks.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on areas such as net retention, agentic adoption, public sector momentum, and guidance prudence, with a neutral to slightly positive tone. They frequently sought clarification on stability and the sustainability of recent gains.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, with Dines and Gupta repeatedly expressing satisfaction with operational progress and customer momentum. Gupta stated, "we feel very comfortable in terms of the guidance that we provided."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone shifted from cautious optimism to more visible confidence, especially regarding the public sector and new product traction.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for revenue and ARR has been raised, reflecting stronger execution and incremental FX tailwinds. The company maintained its prudent outlook, but the language surrounding the public sector was more positive, moving from "transition" to "stabilization."
  • Management's confidence in the Agentic Automation platform increased, with customer examples and usage metrics rising notably from prior quarters.
  • Analysts' questions shifted from seeking clarity on operational stability and restructuring to probing the sustainability of new growth areas, particularly Agentic and public sector momentum.
  • Key financial metrics, such as margins and cash flow, remained strong, with management highlighting continued focus on operational efficiency.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified the variable macroeconomic environment as an ongoing risk, stating, "we continue to maintain a prudent outlook and guide to what we see in front of us."
  • The company noted that while Agentic product adoption is gaining traction, "adoption is still in its early phases, and as such, we don't expect a material top line contribution in fiscal 2026."
  • FX fluctuations were highlighted as having an impact at the time of renewal and contract signing, particularly from European currencies and the yen.
  • Analysts also questioned sustainability of net retention and the impact of macro conditions on customer expansion.

Final Takeaway

UiPath’s Q2 2026 call demonstrated accelerating momentum in Agentic Automation adoption, robust operational execution, and meaningful improvements in revenue guidance and customer engagement. Management’s optimism was supported by notable customer wins, strategic partnerships, and ongoing public sector stabilization, while maintaining a cautious stance on macroeconomic risks and the pace of new product monetization for the remainder of the year.

data.title: Broadcom forecasts $17.4B Q4 revenue as AI semiconductor momentum accelerates with new customer
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Hock Tan, President and CEO, announced "total revenue was a record $16 billion, up 22% year-on-year," citing AI semiconductors and VMware growth as key drivers. "Bookings were extremely strong. And our current consolidated backlog for the company hit a record of $110 billion."
  • Tan stated that Q3 semiconductor revenue reached $9.2 billion, with AI semiconductor revenue of $5.2 billion, up 63% year-on-year, noting this marks "10 consecutive quarters" of robust AI growth.
  • The CEO highlighted the addition of a fourth qualified XPU customer, with over $10 billion in AI rack orders secured. This leads to "the outlook for our fiscal 2026 AI revenue to improve significantly from what we had indicated last quarter."
  • New networking products were introduced, including Tomahawk 5 and Tomahawk 6 switches, and Jericho4 Ethernet fabric router, supporting larger-scale AI clusters. Tan emphasized, "The biggest challenge to deploying larger clusters of compute for generative AI will be in networking."
  • Tan forecasted Q4 AI semiconductor revenue to be approximately $6.2 billion, up 66% year-on-year, and non-AI semiconductor revenue to grow low double digits sequentially to about $4.6 billion.
  • On the software side, Tan revealed the release of VMware Cloud Foundation version 9.0 and noted Q3 infrastructure software revenue of $6.8 billion, with total contract value booked over $8.4 billion.
  • Tan summarized, "continued strength in AI and VMware will drive our guidance for Q4 consolidated revenue to approximately $17.4 billion, up 24% year-on-year."
  • Kirsten Spears, CFO, stated, "Gross margin was 78.4% of revenue in the quarter, better than we originally guided on higher software revenues and product mix within semiconductors."
  • Spears provided further detail: "Q3 operating income was a record $10.5 billion, up 32% from a year ago. Adjusted EBITDA of $10.7 billion or 67% of revenue was above our guidance of 66%."
  • Tan announced an agreement with the Board to continue as CEO through 2030, calling this an "exciting time" for Broadcom.

Outlook

  • Management guided Q4 consolidated revenue to $17.4 billion, with semiconductor revenue of about $10.7 billion and AI semiconductor revenue of $6.2 billion.
  • Infrastructure software revenue is expected to be about $6.7 billion, up 15% year-on-year.
  • Spears indicated, "we expect Q4 consolidated gross margin to be down approximately 70 basis points sequentially, primarily reflecting a higher mix of XPUs and also wireless revenue."
  • The non-GAAP tax rate for Q4 and fiscal year 2025 is expected to remain at 14%.

Financial Results

  • Q3 consolidated revenue reached $16 billion, gross margin was 78.4%, and operating expenses totaled $2 billion.
  • Q3 operating income came in at $10.5 billion, with adjusted EBITDA of $10.7 billion.
  • Semiconductor Solutions revenue was $9.2 billion, with gross margin at approximately 67%. Infrastructure software revenue was $6.8 billion, with gross margin at 93% and operating margin at 77%.
  • Free cash flow was $7 billion, representing 44% of revenue. Inventory rose to $2.2 billion in anticipation of future growth.
  • The company held $10.7 billion in cash and $66.3 billion in gross principal debt at quarter end.
  • Q3 dividends paid totaled $2.8 billion.

Q&A

  • Ross Seymore, Deutsche Bank: Asked about the acceleration of AI/XPU business. Tan responded, "it's the fourth customer that we now add on to our roster...really changes our thinking of what '26 would be."
  • Harlan Sur, JPMorgan: Inquired about non-AI semi cyclical recovery. Tan explained, "non-AI semiconductor is kind of slow to recover...Q4 year-on-year is up maybe low single digits."
  • Vivek Arya, BofA: Sought quantification on 2026 AI growth. Tan replied, "we're seeing the growth rate accelerate as opposed to just remain steady...for 2026."
  • Stacy Rasgon, Bernstein: Asked about the $110 billion backlog. Tan clarified it's "largely driven by AI in terms of growth."
  • Ben Reitzes, Melius: Questioned future prospects beyond current customers. Tan declined to give specifics, stating, "these prospects are real prospects and continue to be very closely engaged."
  • Karl Ackerman, BNP Paribas: Asked about VMware Cloud Foundation adoption. Tan confirmed, "way over 90% has bought VCF," but deployment is ongoing.
  • Christopher Muse, Cantor Fitzgerald: Requested gross margin clarity. Spears said, "TPUs will be going up along with wireless...and our software revenue will be coming up just a bit as well."
  • Joe Moore, Morgan Stanley: Asked about the fourth customer and $10 billion in orders. Tan estimated delivery will be "around...Q3 of our fiscal '26."
  • Harsh Kumar, Piper Sandler: Probed on XPU share. Tan responded, "As they gain confidence...they will have the confidence to keep using a higher and higher percentage of their compute footprint in their own XPUs."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed positive sentiment, congratulating management on results and seeking clarity on growth acceleration, backlog, and new customer momentum.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, highlighting records and future growth: "we continue to make steady progress in growing our AI revenue."
  • During Q&A, management responded with cautious optimism regarding new prospects and acknowledged challenges in non-AI recovery, often avoiding direct forecasts for 2027 and beyond.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed increased optimism around AI and customer expansion, with more direct references to backlog and bookings.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q3 saw record revenue and EBITDA, higher than Q2.
  • AI semiconductor revenue growth accelerated from previous quarter, with the addition of a fourth major customer and improved 2026 outlook.
  • Non-AI segments remain slow to recover, but broadband shows strong momentum.
  • Management tone was more overtly bullish on AI opportunity and customer wins than in Q2, while analysts focused more on AI backlog and future acceleration than in the prior call.
  • Gross margin declined compared to Q2 due to product mix, but operating leverage improved.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted "demand continues to be slow to recover" for non-AI semiconductors, with no V-shaped recovery expected in the near term.
  • Tan cautioned, "as a whole, they are not getting worse...but they are not showing a V-shaped recovery."
  • The CEO stressed the importance of continued innovation and investment to maintain competitive position, especially in the XPU and networking segments.
  • Analysts highlighted the risk of cyclical uncertainty in non-AI business and sought clarity on long-term AI customer prospects.

Final Takeaway

Broadcom management emphasized that robust AI semiconductor and VMware momentum drove record Q3 results and support guidance for a significant Q4 revenue increase to $17.4 billion. The addition of a fourth major XPU customer and record backlog signal an improved outlook for AI-related growth in 2026, while non-AI segments remain subdued but stable. Management's extended leadership continuity and continued investment in both AI and networking infrastructure are positioned as key strengths for sustaining long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: American Outdoor Brands signals 15% Q2 net sales decline outlook amid ongoing tariff and retailer order volatility
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Outdoor Brands (AOUT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Brian Murphy, President, CEO & Director, stated that the company is navigating a dynamic environment marked by evolving tariffs, shifting retailer order patterns, and macroeconomic uncertainty. Murphy emphasized, "This quarter reaffirmed that innovation for us is more than new products. It's a mindset that enabled us to drive stronger point-of-sale performance versus peers across several strategic product categories, a result that is supported by feedback from key retail partners and third-party data." He highlighted that new products represented nearly 29% of net sales in Q1 and pointed to strong consumer pull-through, especially as retailers accelerated orders late in Q4 to get ahead of tariff-induced price changes.

  • Murphy also announced the expansion of the partnership between BUBBA and Major League Fishing, introducing the SCORETRACKER LIVE tournament format to all anglers through the BUBBA app in spring 2026. He described this as an initiative expected to "accelerate our recurring subscription revenue stream, extend BUBBA's reach with anglers of all skill levels and set the stage for near-term product introductions."

  • H. Fulmer, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer, reported, "Net sales in Q1 were $29.7 million compared to $41.6 million in Q1 last year, a decrease of 28.7%." Fulmer noted that, adjusting for the acceleration of retail orders into Q4, traditional channel net sales would have increased 15%. He added, "Gross margin was 46.7%, up 130 basis points compared to Q1 last year." Fulmer further commented on the company's strong balance sheet, with $17.8 million in cash and no debt, and ongoing inventory management efforts to address tariff and seasonal factors.

Outlook

  • Fulmer indicated, "We expect a continuation of a measured ordering cadence as they [retail partners] seek to balance their inventories, optimize pricing and align their purchasing decisions with evolving consumer buying patterns. Accordingly, in the near term, we expect to see a year-over-year decline in net sales for our second quarter of approximately 15%." He also stated, "While we believe it is premature to resume full year guidance, we remain optimistic about the year on the whole."

  • Management did not reinstate full-year guidance, maintaining a cautious approach due to macroeconomic and policy uncertainties.

Financial Results

  • Fulmer reported that net sales for Q1 were $29.7 million, down from $41.6 million in the prior-year period, citing the impact of order acceleration into Q4. E-commerce net sales declined by 35.2% year-over-year, attributed to a large partner adjusting purchasing in response to tariffs. Gross margin improved to 46.7%, up 130 basis points from Q1 last year.

  • GAAP operating expenses were $20.7 million, down from $21.5 million last year. Non-GAAP operating expenses were $18.2 million versus $18.4 million. GAAP EPS was a loss of ($0.54), compared to a loss of ($0.18) last year. Non-GAAP EPS was negative ($0.26), compared to $0.06 last year. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was a loss of $3.1 million versus $2 million in Q1 last year.

  • Inventory increased by $21.1 million, mainly to support seasonal demand and tariff-related strategic stockpiling. Fulmer stated, "We are targeting inventory to remain at roughly $125 million for Q2 and Q3 and then decrease to around $120 million for Q4."

Q&A

  • Douglas Lane, Water Tower Research LLC, asked about excess retailer inventory and tariff management. Fulmer replied that inventory is not considered excess, clarifying that order acceleration was targeted at key categories.

  • Lane also inquired about pricing adjustments and innovation's role. Murphy explained that pricing is just one lever, alongside supplier concessions, product redesigns, and new product launches, stating, "We're continuing to calibrate the price side of things, but that's really also just a function of how well those other levers...are taking hold."

  • Matt Koranda, ROTH Capital Partners, asked about order flow normalization and brand performance. Murphy responded, "Retailers are certainly ordering more cautiously than the POS would suggest...as inventory positions begin to normalize...the two will better align in the future."

  • Koranda also asked about M&A. Murphy noted, "We're still very active in looking at targets...we're not finding a lot of great targets right now. So we're being very patient."

  • Alex Sturnieks, Lake Street Capital Markets, LLC, asked about consumer shifts toward value-oriented products. Murphy said the company's premium or enthusiast segments are holding up, with weaker performance seen at lower price points where the company has less exposure.

  • Sturnieks also asked about gross margin risks and sourcing outside China. Fulmer described ongoing efforts to diversify sourcing and maintain vendor cost concessions, noting sourcing decisions remain fluid based on tariff developments and supplier readiness.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for details on inventory normalization, pricing, and consumer behavior, maintaining a neutral to slightly cautious tone amid macro uncertainty.

  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined stance, emphasizing adaptability and innovation. Phrases like "we remain confident in our long-term strategy" and "we know how to adapt" indicate steady confidence, though the lack of full-year guidance suggests prudence.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts now display heightened caution, with more emphasis on short-term volatility and less clarity for the remainder of the year.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company continues to highlight innovation and supply chain agility, but Q1 2026 reveals a sharper focus on near-term volatility—particularly retailer order cadence and tariff uncertainty.

  • Management reiterated the suspension of annual guidance, consistent with the previous quarter, but added a specific Q2 outlook of a 15% net sales decline.

  • Analysts shifted from exploring growth drivers and post-tariff acceleration in Q4 to probing the duration of demand volatility and the company's response to macro headwinds.

  • Gross margin improvement and inventory build were emphasized this quarter as mitigation strategies.

  • The overall tone moved from celebratory (post-record Q4) to more defensive and measured.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing macroeconomic and tariff policy uncertainty, as well as evolving retailer order patterns, as key challenges.

  • The company is monitoring consumer shifts, potential retailer trade-down, and e-commerce concentration risks.

  • Mitigation strategies include supply chain diversification, dynamic pricing, supplier concessions, and a strong focus on new product launches.

Final Takeaway

American Outdoor Brands’ Q1 2026 results reflect a period of short-term volatility as the company navigates evolving tariffs, shifting retailer purchasing patterns, and macroeconomic headwinds. Management remains focused on leveraging innovation, disciplined inventory management, and strategic partnerships to maintain brand strength and margin resilience. While near-term sales are expected to decline, the company’s agile operating model and robust balance sheet are positioned to capitalize on renewed demand when market conditions stabilize.
